title: Halting genetic diversity loss, from local to international action and policy
abstract: Nature Reviews Biodiversity, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s44358-026-00162-0 The loss of genetic diversity is a major threat to population resilience, posing a challenge for conservation efforts. This Review highlights how the integration of large-scale biodiversity monitoring datasets and population genetics principles can empower biodiversity partners across governance levels to make effective conservation decisions.

title: Pricing nature into sovereign debt
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03101-6 A study that couples ecological–economic scenarios with a machine-learned replica of how nations’ credit ratings are determined shows that even partial losses of just three ecosystem services could materially raise the cost of government borrowing.

title: Latitudinal patterns and environmental correlates of plant root exudation
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03103-4 From an assembled dataset of plant root exudation rate, root traits and environmental covariates, this study shows that the rate of carbon exuded by roots generally increases from high to low latitudes, and soil phosphorus deficiency is the most important influencing factor.

title: Biodiversity loss will decrease the future creditworthiness of nations
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03081-7 Scenario modelling reveals how sovereign credit ratings and the cost of borrowing across 23 nations would change under partial collapses in timber production, wild pollination services and marine fisheries.

title: Different planning scales for renewable expansion yield distinct biodiversity trade-offs in China
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 03 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03097-z In this study, China’s solar and wind expansion through to 2060 is projected under national versus provincial planning scenarios, which represent different centralization levels. Modelling shows that each pathway results in distinct trade-offs with biodiversity, which demonstrates the need for integrated, multiscale siting to align climate and biodiversity conservation goals.

title: Twentieth-century insect declines and partial recoveries driven by changes in land use and climate
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03083-5 Continuous 90-year trends of butterflies and deadwood-dependent beetles show clear mid-century declines and only partial recovery of these taxa. The declines were clearly related to agricultural intensification, and climate change and more biodiversity-friendly management helped in the recovery and stabilization — although butterflies are still far from their original diversity.

title: Alignment-free integration of single-nucleus ATAC-seq across species with sPYce
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03087-1 This paper presents sPYce, a method that integrates single-nucleus ATAC-seq data across species, allowing for comparison of gene regulatory evolution across species.

title: Gene body methylation suppresses intragenic transcription and permits epigenetic inheritance in a cnidarian
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03090-6 Experiments in the cnidarian Nematostella vectensis show a role of gene body methylation in transposable element suppression and that epigenetic inheritance is constrained by chromatin context and transcriptional activity.

title: Ninety-year trends reveal sharpest insect declines in the mid-twentieth century
abstract: Nature Ecology & Evolution, Published online: 02 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41559-026-03074-6 Assessing the magnitude and drivers of recent biodiversity losses requires informative, long-term baseline data. Here the authors reconstruct the temporal trajectories of butterflies and saproxylic beetles in Switzerland from 1930 to 2021, shedding light on the role of climate and land-use change.

title: A hidden pollutant is changing how the world's forests breathe
abstract: A massive global analysis found that nitrogen pollution can either speed up or dramatically slow the natural "breathing" of forest soils, depending on the ecosystem's condition. The results reveal hidden tipping points that could affect how forests store carbon and cope with climate change.

title: This blood-feeding fly sacrifices its sight after finding a host
abstract: Deer keds rely on flight and vision to find a host, but everything changes once they land. After shedding their wings forever, these parasites reduce the activity of key vision-related genes by about half. Scientists believe they are effectively trading sharp eyesight for extra energy that can be used for feeding and reproduction.
